planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root or container shrubs in early spring; can also be planted in fall. Ben Sarek is self-fertile and does not require a cross-pollinator. Plant 5–6 feet apart; the compact habit means Ben Sarek fits in smaller spaces than standard black currant varieties. Prune out wood older than 3 years annually to maintain productivity.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Ben Sarek is a compact black currant cultivar from the Scottish Crop Research Institute's 'Ben' series — the same breeding program that produced Ben Lomond and Ben Tirran. Ben Sarek is the most compact of the Ben series, growing only 3–4 feet tall while producing very heavy crops of large, dark, richly flavored black currant berries in July. The combination of compact size and exceptionally high productivity makes Ben Sarek the best choice for small gardens and urban growing situations where standard currant shrubs are too large. Black currants have a distinctive, intensely aromatic, bittersweet flavor — the basis for cassis liqueur and blackcurrant jam — that is unlike any other common fruit. The very high vitamin C content (more per ounce than almost any other fruit) and exceptionally high anthocyanin levels have made black currant products popular in European functional food and nutraceutical markets.
sources: Scottish Crop Research Institute — Ben Sarek Black Currant Release
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Black Currant Cultivar Evaluation
University of Vermont Extension — Growing Currants in the Northeast

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root or container shrubs in early spring; can also be planted in fall. Consort is self-fertile. This variety is specifically important in US states that allow Ribes cultivation but still recommend white pine blister rust-resistant cultivars. Prune out wood older than 3 years to maintain productivity. In zone 7 Consort performs reliably; zone 8 conditions may reduce dormancy needs.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Consort is a Canadian Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada black currant cultivar bred specifically for resistance to white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola) — a fungal disease that historically devastated currant and gooseberry plantings throughout much of North America and led to federal and state bans on Ribes species in the early 20th century. Consort's white pine blister rust resistance is an important practical attribute in regions where susceptible Ribes cultivars are still regulated or adjacent to white pine forests. The medium-sized, glossy, dark black berries ripen in July with the characteristic intensely aromatic, bittersweet black currant flavor valued for jams, cassis, juice, and wine. Consort grows as a vigorous, upright, productive shrub that is among the more cold-hardy black currant varieties, performing well throughout the northern US and Canada. The rust resistance trait means it can be grown where standard black currant cultivars are prohibited.
sources: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada — Consort Black Currant Release
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Rust-Resistant Ribes Varieties
University of Minnesota Extension — Black Currant for Northern Gardens

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring; in zone 7 plant February–March. Red Pearl is self-fertile and produces two crops per year — a primary crop in late summer (August) and a second crop in fall (September–October). Plant in very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) similar to blueberry culture. The low creeping habit makes Red Pearl excellent as an edible groundcover under acid-loving trees and shrubs.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil similar to blueberry growing conditions; Red Pearl is a Dutch-selected lingonberry (Vaccinium vitis-idaea) cultivar renowned as one of the most productive named varieties, producing two crops of bright red berries per season rather than the single crop of most species plants — the first crop ripens in August and the second in October, significantly extending the fresh lingonberry harvest. The glossy, bright scarlet berries have the characteristic lingonberry flavor: tart, slightly bitter, and astringent when raw, transforming into the quintessential lingonberry jam flavor when sweetened and cooked. Lingonberry jam is the traditional accompaniment to meatballs and other Scandinavian dishes and is a major commercial product in northern Europe. The low-growing, evergreen, spreading habit makes Red Pearl an excellent edible groundcover for shady acidic spots under blueberries, azaleas, rhododendrons, and pines where few other fruiting plants thrive. Spreads slowly by rhizomes to form a dense carpet.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Lingonberry Cultivar Evaluation
University of Minnesota Extension — Red Pearl Lingonberry
USDA ARS — Vaccinium vitis-idaea Cultivar Notes

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Issai is the only commonly available self-fertile Actinidia arguta cultivar, producing fruit without a separate male vine — an important practical advantage for gardeners with space for only one vine. Provide a very sturdy trellis, fence, or pergola; Issai is less vigorous than most hardy kiwi vines but still requires substantial support. In zone 9 it ripens in September–October.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Issai is a Japanese-selected cultivar of hardy kiwi (Actinidia arguta) notable for being reliably self-fertile — unlike the species, which requires separate male and female vines for fruit production, Issai is a self-pollinating selection that sets fruit as a single plant. The small, smooth-skinned, grape-sized kiwiberry fruits ripen in September–October to a dark green with a delicate strawberry-kiwi flavor — eaten whole without peeling, unlike fuzzy kiwis (Actinidia deliciosa) that require peeling. Hardy kiwi fruits are considerably smaller than supermarket fuzzy kiwis but have a thin, edible skin and a sweet, complex, tropical flavor. Issai is less vigorous than most Actinidia arguta plants — a compact 15–20 foot climber rather than the 40+ foot sprawl of standard hardy kiwi — making it more manageable in garden settings. In zone 9 the vines experience mild winters and may have reduced dormancy; provide some shading in the hottest zones.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Hardy Kiwi Cultivar Selection
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Issai Hardy Kiwi
University of California Cooperative Extension — Kiwi Production in Warm Climates

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring; Leikora is a female fruiting cultivar requiring a male pollinator such as 'Pollmix' or 'Hergo-M' within 50–100 feet for fruit set. One male plant can pollinate 6–8 female plants. Sea buckthorn tolerates poor, sandy, gravelly soils — it is one of the most nutrient-poor-soil-tolerant fruiting shrubs due to its nitrogen-fixing root nodules. Avoid planting in wet or clay soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, poor-to-fertile soil — sandy, rocky, and coastal soils are ideal; Leikora is a German-selected female sea buckthorn cultivar bred for commercial berry production, selected at the Bundesanstalt für Züchtungsforschung as one of the heaviest-bearing named female cultivars with large, bright orange-yellow berries that ripen in August–September in dense clusters along the stems. Sea buckthorn berries contain extraordinary concentrations of vitamin C (up to 15 times that of oranges), vitamin E, carotenoids, and omega-7 fatty acids — the nutritional profile has driven substantial commercial interest in sea buckthorn juice, oil capsules, and skincare products. The tart, acidic, tropical-citrus flavor of the fresh berries makes them challenging to eat raw but outstanding processed into juice (often blended with apple or honey to moderate acidity), jam, wine, and dietary supplements. Leikora is a thorny, nitrogen-fixing, drought-tolerant shrub adapted to harsh, exposed sites where few other fruiting plants survive.
sources: Bundesanstalt für Züchtungsforschung — Leikora Sea Buckthorn Selection
University of Saskatchewan — Sea Buckthorn Cultivar Guide
USDA ARS — Hippophae rhamnoides Named Cultivars
